_note: Running patch doc — editorial rewrites, approved 1-by-1. Each entry lists ONLY changed fields + id. Merge into concepts.json by id once batch of 10 is approved.

approved.plain: Almost everything you see gets filtered out instantly, unless it hits one of five triggers: scary, strange, sexy, unexpectedly free, or familiar through repetition. Most marketing and personal branding fails because it doesn't trigger any of the five.
approved.analogy: On a crowded Oxford Street, only the guy in the hot dog costume actually registers among the three thousand faces you pass.

approved.plain: People are drawn to individuals who love something with unfiltered intensity. It's not the thing itself that's compelling; it's the depth of their commitment. When someone stops apologizing for what excites them, it gives others permission to do the same.
approved.analogy: Rally fans stand for hours in freezing Scottish rain, drenched and grinning, just to watch cars flash past for thirty seconds.

approved.hook: Conform and resent yourself, or stand out and unsettle everyone else.
approved.analogy: At Thanksgiving dinner, you announce you're quitting law school, and your father's fork stops halfway to his mouth.

approved.plain: A real apology names exactly what you did, admits the impact, and stops there, no 'because you...' clause attached. Add one, and the whole thing falls apart.
approved.analogy: You say, 'I shouldn't have snapped at you, and I know it stung' instead of 'I only snapped because you pushed me first.'
A thermostat reads the room, compares it to your target, and fires the heater the instant the gap widens.

approved.plain: Accusations of change usually mean you stopped fitting the position someone else assigned you. That's not malice, it's that they've rarely watched anyone grow, so they don't really know how to find the right words to respond with.
approved.analogy: You order a soda water at the party, and your old friend says, 'Since when are you no fun?'

approved.hook: Nice guys chase being liked, good men chase being worthy.
approved.analogy: When a friend forgets to repay $200, the nice guy laughs it off, and the good man just asks for it back.

approved.plain: A quick break doesn't do much, your body is still full of stress hormones and can't think straight yet. What actually works is stepping fully away from whatever set you off and waiting until that passes before you try to talk again.
approved.analogy: Your partner says 'give me a minute,' then storms back ninety seconds later, still red-faced and ready to keep fighting.

approved.plain: Most companies in an industry just copy whatever's already working elsewhere instead of testing new ideas themselves. If you're the only one running real experiments and learning from what fails, you build a lead competitors can't just copy by watching you from outside.
approved.analogy: Every two weeks, Gym Launch dropped $50K testing sales tactics so licensees could skip straight to the 30% that worked.

approved.hook: A tech-enabled team built for one goal: an eight-to-nine-figure exit.
approved.plain: This kind of business runs about thirty people, with real IP and a repeatable go-to-market engine that a strategic buyer or private equity firm will actually pay for. You're tracking LTV, CAC, and growth metrics constantly, because the goal is a liquidity event, not just steady annual profit.
approved.analogy: A 34-person SaaS company hits ten million in recurring revenue, then sells to a public company for fifty million, seven years after it started.
